mnemonics.title: Vector Space Checklist
mnemonics.content: VECTOR SPACE: Remember the 8 axioms with 'C-C-A-D-Z' - Closure, Commutativity, Associativity, Distributivity, Additive identity, Zero vector, Additive inverse, Scalar multiplication

mnemonics.title: Linear Transformation Test
mnemonics.content: LINEAR: Use 'ADD SCALE' - T(u+v) = T(u)+T(v) [Addition preserved] and T(αv) = αT(v) [Scalar multiplication preserved]

mnemonics.title: Basis Requirements
mnemonics.content: BASIS = B: Both linearly Independent and Span the space

acronyms.usage: Matrix form where each leading 1 is the only non-zero entry in its column
Square matrix with 1s on diagonal and 0s elsewhere
Method of factoring a matrix into lower and upper triangular matrices

visualTips.visualization: Think of arrows starting from origin: length = magnitude, arrow head = direction, can represent displacement or force in physics
Place vectors head-to-tail in sequence; the result is the arrow from start to end (triangle/parallelogram method)
Picture a matrix as a machine that takes a vector input and transforms it to a vector output - stretching, rotating, or projecting
Span of 1 vector = line through origin; span of 2 non-parallel vectors = plane; span of 3 linearly independent vectors = entire 3D space
Vectors are independent if removing any one reduces the span; think of legs of a tripod - each is essential

analogies.analogy: Like a playground where you can add children (vectors) and change their ages (scalars), but they must stay within the playground boundaries
Like a photo filter that applies the same rule to every pixel - consistently stretching, rotating, or skewing the entire image
Like coordinate system - just as X,Y,Z axes let us locate any point, basis vectors let us express any vector uniquely
Dimension is like degrees of freedom - in 2D you need 2 numbers, in 3D you need 3 to specify a location completely
Like function composition - applying transformation B after transformation A, combined into single operation AB
